color_semantics.CRIMSON: classical expectation / boson enhancement (distinguishable particles, symmetric wavefunction, diagonal ridge, no node)

metadata.note: Card 07. Exclusions: no Slater determinant, no helium exchange-integral J/K algebra, no spin-statistics-theorem proof.

beats.narration_text: Two electrons stay farther apart than chance predicts. No force pushes them. Nothing in the Hamiltonian changes. The wavefunction does it.

card.headline: Why Two Electrons Avoid Each Other With No Force

beats.narration_text: For two distinguishable particles, the joint probability is simply |ψ₁(x₁)|²|ψ₂(x₂)|². No preferred diagonal. No avoided region. Each particle is free to be anywhere.
Electrons are identical. If we can't say which is which, does the joint probability change? What forces the statistics?
Identical fermions require an antisymmetric wavefunction: swap the two particles and the sign flips. Put them at the same x — ψ(x,x) = −ψ(x,x) — which forces ψ = 0 there.
That zero is a node that runs along the entire diagonal x₁ = x₂ of the joint-probability surface. The two electrons cannot sit at the same point — even without any repulsive force.
The mechanism: antisymmetry carves a permanent node.
Bosons are symmetric — swap them, sign stays the same. Their diagonal becomes a ridge. Bosons bunch; fermions avoid. Opposite statistics from opposite signs.
With no interaction term turned on, fermions average a larger separation than distinguishable particles; bosons average a smaller one. The exchange correlation is real — only the symmetry creates it.

This is the engine behind the periodic table. Without antisymmetry, every electron would pile into the 1s orbital. Antisymmetry forces electrons into higher shells — atoms have size, matter has structure.

props.headline: This is the engine behind the periodic table. Without antisymmetry, every…

beats.narration_text: The helium singlet-triplet energy difference — orthohelium vs parahelium — is the same effect. Different symmetry sectors, different probabilities of finding electrons close, different Coulomb energies. No extra force.

Two non-interacting particles in a 1D box, each in a different orbital. Distinguishable: ⟨(x₁−x₂)²⟩ = baseline. Fermions: baseline + exchange integral > baseline. Bosons: baseline − exchange integral < baseline. No Hamiltonian change — only the symmetry postulate.

What forces the statistics when electrons have no force between them? Antisymmetry writes a zero along the diagonal — they cannot share a point. One sign separates matter's structure from its collapse. QUANTUM MECHANICS.

card.headline: Antisymmetry writes the node. No force needed.
